flashcards.front: What is a frame in the context of memory management?
flashcards.back: A frame is a fixed-size block of physical memory in a paging system. Physical memory is divided into equal-sized frames, and each frame can hold exactly one page of a process.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of frame allocation in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Frame allocation determines how physical memory frames are distributed among processes. It decides how many frames each process receives, which directly impacts system performance, page fault rates, and thrashing.
In equal allocation, each process receives the same number of frames regardless of its size or priority. For example, if there are m frames and n processes, each gets m/n frames.

In proportional allocation, frames are distributed based on process size. A larger process gets more frames than a smaller process. If process size is s and total memory is S, the allocation is (s/S) × m frames.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between global and local frame allocation?
flashcards.back: Global allocation allows all processes to compete for any available frame; one process gaining frames affects others. Local allocation restricts each process to its allocated frames; a process cannot use frames belonging to another process.

flashcards.front: What is thrashing in frame allocation?
flashcards.back: Thrashing occurs when a process spends more time paging (swapping pages in and out) than executing, due to insufficient frames. It leads to severe degradation in CPU utilization and system performance.

flashcards.front: What is the working set model in frame allocation?
flashcards.back: The working set is the set of pages a process is actively using. The OS allocates enough frames to hold the working set. If the working set grows, more frames are needed; if it shrinks, frames can be reclaimed.

flashcards.front: What is page fault frequency (PFF) in frame allocation?
flashcards.back: PFF measures how often a process experiences page faults. The OS monitors PFF and adjusts frame allocation: if PFF is too high, allocate more frames; if too low, reclaim excess frames.

flashcards.front: What is the minimum number of frames required for a process?
flashcards.back: The minimum number of frames is determined by the maximum number of pages a single instruction can reference (its address spread). This ensures an instruction can execute without causing multiple page faults.

In priority-based allocation, higher-priority processes receive more frames to reduce their page faults. This helps critical processes run efficiently, possibly at the expense of lower-priority processes.

flashcards.front: What is local page replacement in frame allocation context?
flashcards.back: Local replacement restricts a process to replacing only pages from its own allocated frames. The process cannot use frames belonging to other processes.

flashcards.front: What is global page replacement in frame allocation context?
flashcards.back: Global replacement allows any process to replace any page in any available frame. This makes allocation more flexible but can cause unpredictable performance for individual processes.

flashcards.front: How does degree of multiprogramming affect frame allocation?
flashcards.back: As the degree of multiprogramming increases (more processes in memory), each process receives fewer frames on average. This can increase page faults and eventually lead to thrashing if frames become too scarce.

flashcards.front: What happens when too few frames are allocated to a process?
flashcards.back: With too few frames, a process experiences high page fault rates. The process spends excessive time on I/O operations, CPU utilization drops, and the system may begin to thrash.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between allocation strategy and system throughput?
flashcards.back: An efficient frame allocation strategy maximizes system throughput by balancing frame distribution. Too much fragmentation (too many small allocations) wastes memory; too few processes reduce CPU utilization.
